text: This article, based on ethnographic fieldwork, analyses the institution of the Pattani Central Mosque in Southern Thailand by offering a counter-perspective to the common notion in the international press that mosques in the South are training grounds for separatist insurgents and breeding grounds for violent extremism and, therefore, sites of Islamic extremism. Since the Kru-ze Mosque incident of April 28, 2004, which was heavily reported in the Thai and international media, concerns were raised over issues of Islamic education, mosque administration, and human rights approached by the leaders and members of the mosque as well as over the future of Thai Islam in the war-torn South. Focussing particularly on the levels of religious education propagated at the mosque along moderate and conservative lines as well as the sermons delivered by its imam and other preachers over the course of a full Ramadhan festival and during the period of civil uprising against the Abhisit government, this paper identifies a range of factors that indicate that the Pattani Central Mosque has succeeded in achieving a delicate administrative power-sharing that respects traditional Islamic religious instruction and the teaching of religious values such as peace, justice and unity, and so functions as a sanctuary of faith within which radicalism of any sort is kept to a minimum.
